name: Eczema / Contact Dermatitis
ontario_ma_scope: Atopic dermatitis IS in Ontario MA scope (Oct 2023). Allergic contact dermatitis (ACD) is NOT — pharmacists cannot prescribe for new-onset ACD requiring patch testing. This module focuses on atopic dermatitis.

key_questions: Atopic dermatitis (personal/family history of atopy, chronic/relapsing, typical distribution)?
Allergic contact dermatitis (ACD): exposure to latex, nickel, fragrances, preservatives, hair dye?
Irritant contact dermatitis (ICD): repeated exposure to water, detergents, solvents (most common — healthcare workers)?
Distribution matches exposure (hands for ICD/ACD; generalized for atopic)?
red_flags: Secondary bacterial infection (weeping/yellow crusting, fever) → S. aureus
Eczema herpeticum (widespread punched-out vesicles in eczema) → HSV emergency — urgent referral
Erythroderma (≥90% BSA) = emergency referral to ER
Moderate-severe uncontrolled eczema not responding to optimized topical therapy → refer for dupilumab (Dupixent) or JAK inhibitor consideration
ddx: Seborrheic dermatitis (scalp, nasolabial folds, greasy scale)
Atopic dermatitis: personal/family history of atopy, flexural distribution, chronic pruritus
Irritant contact dermatitis: exposure history (water, detergents, solvents), no sensitization, common on hands, resolves with avoidance
Allergic contact dermatitis: sensitization required, patch test positive, resolves with allergen avoidance (nickel, fragrance, methylisothiazolinone most common)

first_line.name: Emollient (Cornerstone)
first_line.drugs: Cetaphil cream | CeraVe moisturizing cream | Eucerin | Aveeno — apply immediately after bathing ('soak and seal')
first_line.notes: Liberal emollient use is THE cornerstone of eczema management. Apply to entire body immediately after bathing (within 3 minutes) to seal in moisture. Use unscented, fragrance-free products. Apply liberally QID. More is better. Can reduce topical steroid requirement significantly. Fingertip unit (FTU) guide: 1 FTU (from fingertip to first crease) covers approximately 2 palm areas of adult skin. Face = 2.5 FTU; one arm = 3 FTU; one hand = 1 FTU; trunk front = 7 FTU.
first_line.preferred_when: All patients with eczema — baseline maintenance

first_line.name: Topical Corticosteroid
first_line.drugs: Hydrocortisone 1–2.5% (face/folds/children) | Desonide 0.05% (sensitive areas) | Betamethasone valerate 0.05–0.1% (trunk/limbs) | Mometasone 0.1% (trunk/limbs)
first_line.notes: Match potency to site: LOW (hydrocortisone 1–2.5%, desonide 0.05%) for face, eyelids, groin, axilla; MODERATE (betamethasone valerate 0.05–0.1%, mometasone 0.1%) for trunk and extremities. Apply fingertip unit amount to affected areas BID during flare. ⚠️ High-potency TCS (e.g., betamethasone dipropionate 0.05%) is generally OUTSIDE Ontario MA prescribing scope. MA TCS prescribing is limited to low-to-mid potency: hydrocortisone 1–2.5%, desonide 0.05%, betamethasone valerate 0.05–0.1%, mometasone 0.1%. Refer for high-potency. Do not use high-potency on face/skin folds. Proactive/maintenance therapy: apply low-potency TCS 2×/week to previously affected areas (even when clear) to prevent flares — AAD 2023 strong recommendation.

second_line.name: Topical Calcineurin Inhibitor (TCI)
second_line.drugs: Tacrolimus 0.03%/0.1% ointment (Protopic) BID | Pimecrolimus 1% cream (Elidel) BID
second_line.notes: Physician/NP-prescribed only — NOT within Ontario MA prescribing scope. Refer for TCI consideration in patients failing TCS. Non-steroidal option for face, eyelids, skin folds (areas where TCS causes atrophy). Burning/stinging on application (transient, improves). Useful for maintenance in sensitive areas. FDA boxed warning still in place; however, AAD 2023 guideline conditionally recommends TCIs noting that long-term data (>10 years) shows no increased population-level cancer risk. Maintain precaution; counsel patients on the warning while reassuring about evidence.

second_line.name: Crisaborole (Eucrisa)
second_line.notes: Non-steroidal PDE4 inhibitor; Health Canada approved for mild-to-moderate atopic dermatitis ≥2 years. Steroid-sparing option for sensitive areas (face, skin folds). Transient application site stinging possible.

non_pharm: Identify and avoid triggers: soaps, detergents, fragrances, wool, extreme temperatures, stress, sweating
Lukewarm (not hot) bath/shower — brief; pat dry (do NOT rub)
Bleach bath 2× weekly (1/2 cup bleach in full tub) — reduces S. aureus colonization
Keep nails trimmed short (reduces scratch damage)
Fingertip unit (FTU) guide: 1 FTU (from fingertip to first crease) covers approximately 2 palm areas of adult skin. Face = 2.5 FTU; one arm = 3 FTU; one hand = 1 FTU; trunk front = 7 FTU.
Proactive/maintenance therapy: apply low-potency TCS 2×/week to previously affected areas (even when clear) to prevent flares — AAD 2023 strong recommendation
Wet wrap therapy for moderate-severe flares: apply TCS, cover with damp tubular bandage or cotton pyjamas, then dry layer on top × 2–6h or overnight
Oral antihistamines are NOT effective for itch in atopic dermatitis (AAD 2023 — itch mediated by IL-31, not histamine). Sedating antihistamines (e.g., diphenhydramine) may help sleep only.
refer_when: Moderate-to-severe AD not responding to optimized topical therapy → refer for dupilumab (Dupixent) or JAK inhibitor (abrocitinib, upadacitinib) consideration
Erythroderma (≥90% BSA) → emergency referral to ER
Suspected allergic contact dermatitis (ACD) — patch testing needed (NOT within pharmacist MA scope)
Secondary infected eczema not responding to 7 days of antibiotics

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema). OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
Canadian Dermatology Association (CDA). Atopic Dermatitis Guidelines — Canadian Consensus (2023).

Sidbury R, et al. Guidelines of care for the management of atopic dermatitis in adults with phototherapy and systemic therapies. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2023;89(2):e49–e70. (AAD, full guideline series 2023)
NICE. Atopic eczema in under 12s: diagnosis and management. NICE guideline [CG57] (2007, updated 2023); Eczema — atopic: CKS (2023).
